video_title: AI Mania Shows Cracks as Asia Sells Off and War Risks Spike | Insight with Haslinda Amin 06/08/2026
gist: An Asia-wide AI/tech selloff (Kospi -8%, circuit breaker tripped, Samsung and SK Hynix -7%) sparked by a hot US jobs report and rising Fed/BOK/BOJ hike odds is read by Barings and Bloomberg reporters as a leverage-driven positioning unwind rather than a fundamental break in the AI story. The episode layers in a structurally higher inflation regime, Iran-Israel war risk to oil supply, and India's coordinated RBI/government 'bazooka' aiming to pull in ~$50bn to defend the rupee.

themes.theme: Asia tech selloff as a leverage unwind, not a fundamental break

insights.claim: The Asian tech rout is a leverage-amplified unwind of speculative retail accumulation (especially via leveraged ETFs) rather than a fundamental deterioration, and represents a buying opportunity into names where earnings keep rising.
insights.speaker: Tim Moe, Goldman Sachs chief APAC equity strategist
insights.so_what: Frames the ~8% Kospi drop as a dip to buy; watch margin/leverage data to time when the unwind is exhausted before adding.
insights.quote: It's a technical correction. This is an opportunity, whether it's a day or a week away, to be increasing exposure to these areas where we know the earnings are continuing to go up.

insights.claim: Higher US rate expectations are driving a rotation out of speculative growth into value, and Asia outside of technology has little cyclical exposure, concentrating the selloff in tech-heavy Korea and Taiwan.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens, Bloomberg Markets reporter
insights.so_what: Explains why Korea/Taiwan are the epicenter; rate-driven rotation can persist until rate path stabilizes.
insights.quote: People got too long to fast and despite the fundamentals more or less being solid, you're getting a lot of that positioning unwinding.

insights.claim: Korean margin debt is at a record high and leveraged ETFs (listed in Hong Kong and newly in Korea) create rebalancing flows that amplify both losses and gains, making the Korean market the most volatile in the world.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg managing editor for Asia equities
insights.so_what: Structural leverage means sharp two-way moves; reflexive ETF rebalancing is a risk indicator to monitor around big down days.
insights.quote: Margin loans taken out by retail investors to bet on stocks is at record high right now.

insights.claim: The Kospi is up roughly 80% year-to-date even after the selloff, making it the most volatile major market over the past 12 months and demanding a multi-year holding horizon to ride out drawdowns.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings global head of public assets
insights.so_what: Sizing matters: investors need a 3-year horizon and tolerance for double-digit swings to hold Korean tech.
insights.quote: It's up 80% this year in spite of the sell off... you've just picked probably the most volatile stock market in the world.

insights.claim: Korea was wavering between net buying and net selling by foreigners as the market suddenly cheapened, meaning the currency may end the day flat despite an 8% equity plunge — a notable change in the prior equity/FX correlation.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens, Bloomberg Markets reporter
insights.so_what: A breakdown in the usual stocks-down/won-down link signals foreigners may be dip-buying; watch flow data for confirmation of a bottom.

themes.theme: AI as an unhedgeable structural bet and capital-markets magnet

insights.claim: AI is a future-state bet on earnings and industrial footprint that cannot be hedged, so investors are effectively all-in and must accept large oscillations, with winners and losers still to be flushed out.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Treat AI exposure as a non-hedgeable secular position; size for volatility and expect dispersion between winners and losers.
insights.quote: So we are all in on AI. There is no way to hedge this.

insights.claim: Companies must plough hundreds of billions into AI and will tap both bond and equity markets, creating ongoing oscillation between pools of capital as issuance ramps.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Expect heavy corporate debt and equity issuance to fund AI capex; a setup for credit spread and supply pressure.

insights.claim: Unlike the dot-com bubble, every industry and company is already actively deploying AI in their own operations, which the speaker argues makes the current cycle fundamentally different from 2000.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Pushback on bubble framing; broad real-world adoption supports durability of AI demand even through corrections.
insights.quote: we can see that that's slightly different than the.com bubble which people like to make comparisons with.

insights.claim: The three large upcoming IPOs (including a SpaceX/space IPO) will suck in capital due to technical pressure on active and passive managers to own them, potentially starving smaller IPOs coming behind them.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Index-inclusion mechanics could concentrate IPO demand into mega-deals; small-cap IPO pipeline may underperform.
insights.quote: I think there's also going to be a lot of technical pressures for active and passive managers to own that name.

themes.theme: Structurally higher inflation and the rates backdrop

insights.claim: AI is inflationary both near-term and structurally, and combined with tariff impacts and event-driven energy shocks is pushing the rate curve higher.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Supports positioning for a structurally higher inflation regime; bearish duration, watch term premium.
insights.quote: I is impregnating sovereigns and and speculation around inflation... The eye is inflationary.

insights.claim: The market prices roughly a 50% chance of a 50bp Fed hike by year-end, but aggressive Fed tightening would not address oil-driven inflation, AI capex, or tariff pass-through and instead risks demand destruction already visible today.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Skeptical the Fed over-tightens; suggests the rate-hike fear driving the selloff may be overdone if growth wobbles.

insights.claim: US job-market strength is being fueled by redeployment of accumulated household wealth from a 5-year market run, even as AI is already causing job contraction in financial services.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Wealth-effect-driven labor resilience is fragile if confidence erodes; AI-driven white-collar job cuts are an emerging signal.

themes.theme: Oil supply risk from Iran-Israel and depleted buffers

insights.claim: Markets are underpricing oil upside because supply buffers are being depleted; reopening the Strait of Hormuz would take at least 30 days even with an agreement, and rebuilding oil infrastructure could take up to six years.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Asymmetric upside risk to oil into winter; restocking date (June 30) and European gas ~15% below normal are catalysts to watch.
insights.quote: It does feel like the markets are underpriced, saying that oil prices can actually surge from here.

insights.claim: European gas supplies are about 15% below normal for the time of year and June 30 is a key global energy restocking date, with an El Nino year potentially providing a milder winter that could keep the energy shock manageable.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: Winter weather and China's opaque reserve position are the swing factors between a manageable and severe energy shock.

insights.claim: Central banks are treating event-driven energy inflation as 'transitory' and betting on a snap-back, a complacency the speaker warns may not hold given the supply timeline.
insights.speaker: Martin Horn, Barings
insights.so_what: If the oil shock persists, central banks may be forced to react late; a risk to the 'transitory' market consensus.
insights.quote: They're assuming the central banks will treat this as truly transitory. You know, that phrase that they won't use again?

themes.theme: Asian FX defense without rate hikes

insights.claim: Asian governments (Korea, India, earlier Indonesia) are defending currencies via jawboning — regulators calling FX desks to deter one-way bets and bond-trading incentives — rather than rate hikes, which only buys a pause.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens, Bloomberg Markets reporter
insights.so_what: Verbal/regulatory FX intervention is a temporary pause; eventual BOK/BOJ rate hikes are the real tool, with equity-market pain as the trade-off.
insights.quote: If you are sitting on a FX trading desk and you get a call directly from the Korean government, you will take a break for the day.

insights.claim: The Korean won is at its weakest since 2009, pressured by record equity outflows, US trade-negotiation concerns, and Korea's heavy reliance on energy imports.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg managing editor for Asia equities
insights.so_what: An excessively weak won eventually undermines the bull case for Korean exporters and stocks; a key threshold to watch.

themes.theme: Indonesia: crisis of confidence from policy uncertainty

insights.claim: Indonesia is the world's worst-performing market — down ~39% from highs and ~16% below where Prabowo took office, with the rupiah down ~13% — driven by export-control execution problems, MSCI free-float concerns, and FX reserves at the lowest since 2018.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens, Bloomberg Markets reporter
insights.so_what: A crisis of confidence; mid-month MSCI index review and June 18 Bank Indonesia meeting are catalysts for clarity.
insights.quote: The market's down around 39% from the highs, and now is about 16% lower than when he started.

insights.claim: Indonesia chose the wrong time to restrict its commodity exports because, lacking any AI exposure or incoming foreign investment, it removed the very support (export earnings) that funded the rupiah and Prabowo's ~$20bn free-meal program.
insights.speaker: Anthony Stevens, Bloomberg Markets reporter
insights.so_what: Twin pressure of falling export revenue and costly social spending forces heavy FX-reserve drawdown; bearish for IDR until policy clarity.
insights.quote: If you don't have any eye and you don't have any incoming foreign investment.

themes.theme: India's coordinated 'bazooka' to defend the rupee

insights.claim: India's combined RBI/government measures (diaspora/NRI deposit scheme, relaxed FPI limits, expanded bond access, tax cuts, ECB hedging concessions for PSUs) could pull in more than $50bn and plug most of a tracked ~$70bn balance-of-payments deficit, much of it front-loaded before September 30.
insights.speaker: Sonal Varma, Nomura chief economist India & Asia ex-Japan
insights.so_what: A credible BoP-deficit plug supports the rupee near-term; front-loaded inflows by Sept 30 are a tradable timeline.
insights.quote: We do think cumulatively, uh, can get more than 50 billion USD.

insights.claim: Rate hikes are NOT the effective tool to stabilize the rupee — India's 2013 hike to defend the currency backfired because much of India's capital is growth capital — so the non-monetary inflow measures are the right call.
insights.speaker: Sonal Varma, Nomura
insights.so_what: Counters the consensus that RBI must hike to defend the rupee; the bar for hikes is a broad inflation generalization, not FX.
insights.quote: the 2013 interest rate hike to defend the currency actually backfired. So I don't think interest rate hikes will actually help stabilize the currency.

insights.claim: The real structural challenge is FDI repatriation and outward direct investment (~$30bn outflow), not gross inflows which run over $90bn over 12 months — meaning currency stability beyond 2026 requires deeper reform.
insights.speaker: Sonal Varma, Nomura
insights.so_what: The 'bazooka' is a 2026 fix; sustainable rupee strength needs ease-of-doing-business reform and AI-adoption integration, a 2027+ watch item.

insights.claim: Nomura assumes Brent averages $95-100/bbl this fiscal year, yielding a manageable current-account deficit around 2.4% of GDP, but a permanently higher oil price would create GOP challenges next calendar year.
insights.speaker: Sonal Varma, Nomura
insights.so_what: Oil at $95-100 is the breakpoint for India's external math; a sustained move above it shifts the rupee/fiscal outlook negative.

insights.claim: India's Q1 GDP grew faster than expected on private investment and fixed capital formation, but consumption is tempering and economists peg up to 50bp of RBI rate hikes ahead amid rising inflation and a forecast subpar monsoon.
insights.speaker: Bloomberg South Asia economy editor
insights.so_what: Growth/inflation tension plus monsoon risk means rate hikes could eat into growth; watch June data and trade-deal progress.
